channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Agency warns punitive second-home taxes won’t free up housing
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes unlikely to boost housing supply, experts warn

Sharp increases in council tax premiums for second homeowners across Scotland may fail to address housing shortages whilst potentially becoming an ineffective revenue tool, according to property market analysts. With local authorities implementing wildly varying surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – concerns are growing that these measures represent political gestures rather than practical housing solutions.

Questionable impact on housing availability

Property specialists highlight that the disproportionate targeting of second homes – which represent a tiny fraction of housing stock in some areas – suggests these policies may have minimal effect on overall availability. In Midlothian, for instance, only 35 properties fall into the second-home category, with just two facing the full 600% combined tax burden when including standard council tax.

David Alexander of DJ Alexander Ltd commented: "When you examine the actual numbers affected, it becomes clear these measures are more about optics than outcomes. The notion that penalising a handful of property owners will meaningfully impact a systemic housing shortage doesn't withstand scrutiny."

Consultation concerns and implementation issues

Critics have raised questions about the genuineness of council consultations on these tax hikes, with Edinburgh's recent process being described as "a box-ticking exercise". The city's consultation document reportedly stated the increase would proceed regardless of feedback, with implementation planned for October.

Alexander noted: "When authorities announce consultations whilst simultaneously confirming the policy will proceed unchanged, it undermines trust in the process. This approach risks alienating property owners whilst failing to deliver tangible housing benefits."

With some homeowners potentially facing annual tax bills approaching £28,000 on higher-value properties, industry observers suggest these measures may simply displace second-home ownership rather than converting properties into primary residences. The long-term effectiveness of such policies in addressing Scotland's housing challenges remains uncertain.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes unlikely to boost housing supply, experts warn

Sharp increases in council tax premiums for second homeowners across Scotland may fail to address housing shortages whilst potentially becoming an ineffective revenue tool, according to property market analysts. With local authorities implementing wildly varying surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – concerns are growing that these measures represent political gestures rather than practical housing solutions.

Questionable impact on housing availability

Property specialists highlight that the disproportionate targeting of second homes – which represent a tiny fraction of housing stock in some areas – suggests these policies may have minimal effect on overall availability. In Midlothian, for instance, only 35 properties fall into the second-home category, with just two facing the full 600% combined tax burden when including standard council tax.

David Alexander of DJ Alexander Ltd commented: "When you examine the actual numbers affected, it becomes clear these measures are more about optics than outcomes. The notion that penalising a handful of property owners will meaningfully impact a systemic housing shortage doesn't withstand scrutiny."

Consultation concerns and implementation issues

Critics have raised questions about the genuineness of council consultations on these tax hikes, with Edinburgh's recent process being described as "a box-ticking exercise". The city's consultation document reportedly stated the increase would proceed regardless of feedback, with implementation planned for October.

Alexander noted: "When authorities announce consultations whilst simultaneously confirming the policy will proceed unchanged, it undermines trust in the process. This approach risks alienating property owners whilst failing to deliver tangible housing benefits."

With some homeowners potentially facing annual tax bills approaching £28,000 on higher-value properties, industry observers suggest these measures may simply displace second-home ownership rather than converting properties into primary residences. The long-term effectiveness of such policies in addressing Scotland's housing challenges remains uncertain.

item.title: BTL landlord earnings surge as tenant demand remains strong
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Buy-to-let landlords have seen a significant boost in rental incomes during the first quarter of 2026, according to fresh research from Pegasus Insight. The latest Landlord Trends survey reveals that average annual rental earnings reached £89,000, marking a £14,000 increase from the previous quarter and a £16,000 rise compared to the same period last year.

Strong Tenant Demand Supports Landlord Earnings

The rebound in landlord incomes follows a temporary dip in late 2025, with current figures showing a 20% year-on-year growth. Analysts suggest this upward trend stems from robust tenant demand, high occupancy rates and longer tenancy durations rather than rental price hikes alone.

Supporting data from Pegasus Insight's Tenant Trends research indicates renters are staying put for extended periods. The average tenant has lived in rented accommodation for approximately eight years, with over five years spent in their current property. Notably, 76% expressed satisfaction with their landlord or letting agent's service.

Stability Benefits Both Landlords and Tenants

The research highlights how longer tenancies create mutually beneficial arrangements. For landlords, reduced tenant turnover means lower void periods and fewer administrative costs. Tenants, meanwhile, enjoy greater housing stability without frequent relocation pressures.

Mark Long, managing director of Pegasus Insight, commented: "These findings demonstrate the enduring strength of the private rental sector's fundamentals. While regulatory changes dominate discussions, the landlord-tenant relationship continues showing remarkable resilience."

The report also notes the average landlord portfolio value has grown to £1.69 million, reflecting sustained property price appreciation across the sector. With two-thirds of tenants intending to remain in their current homes after their tenancy agreements expire, the outlook suggests continued stability for buy-to-let investors.

Whilst acknowledging ongoing challenges around affordability and compliance costs, the research paints an encouraging picture for landlords navigating today's property market. The combination of strong tenant demand, lengthening tenancies and rising portfolio values may offer reassurance to those committed to the private rental sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Buy-to-let landlords have seen a significant boost in rental incomes during the first quarter of 2026, according to fresh research from Pegasus Insight. The latest Landlord Trends survey reveals that average annual rental earnings reached £89,000, marking a £14,000 increase from the previous quarter and a £16,000 rise compared to the same period last year.

Strong Tenant Demand Supports Landlord Earnings

The rebound in landlord incomes follows a temporary dip in late 2025, with current figures showing a 20% year-on-year growth. Analysts suggest this upward trend stems from robust tenant demand, high occupancy rates and longer tenancy durations rather than rental price hikes alone.

Supporting data from Pegasus Insight's Tenant Trends research indicates renters are staying put for extended periods. The average tenant has lived in rented accommodation for approximately eight years, with over five years spent in their current property. Notably, 76% expressed satisfaction with their landlord or letting agent's service.

Stability Benefits Both Landlords and Tenants

The research highlights how longer tenancies create mutually beneficial arrangements. For landlords, reduced tenant turnover means lower void periods and fewer administrative costs. Tenants, meanwhile, enjoy greater housing stability without frequent relocation pressures.

Mark Long, managing director of Pegasus Insight, commented: "These findings demonstrate the enduring strength of the private rental sector's fundamentals. While regulatory changes dominate discussions, the landlord-tenant relationship continues showing remarkable resilience."

The report also notes the average landlord portfolio value has grown to £1.69 million, reflecting sustained property price appreciation across the sector. With two-thirds of tenants intending to remain in their current homes after their tenancy agreements expire, the outlook suggests continued stability for buy-to-let investors.

Whilst acknowledging ongoing challenges around affordability and compliance costs, the research paints an encouraging picture for landlords navigating today's property market. The combination of strong tenant demand, lengthening tenancies and rising portfolio values may offer reassurance to those committed to the private rental sector.

item.title: One in five landlords don’t trust agents with client money
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that nearly 20% of landlords in England have concerns about letting agents' handling of rental payments and deposits. The study by The Letting Partnership, which surveyed 890 landlords, highlights ongoing trust issues in the rental sector despite most property owners generally trusting their agents.

Landlords seek greater financial transparency

The survey found that 19% of landlords lack confidence in agents' management of client money, including rent collection and deposit holding. This suggests that while the majority of working relationships function smoothly, a significant minority of property investors remain uneasy about financial processes.

Communication gaps appear to be a key factor, with 34% of respondents stating their agents fail to clearly explain compliance procedures or professional standards. The research also uncovered low awareness of voluntary industry checks, as 76% of landlords didn't know some agents undergo independent assessments of their financial and operational practices.

Independent verification could boost confidence

Nearly all surveyed landlords (99%) said they would feel more confident in an agent that could demonstrate independently verified compliance. This strong response indicates that transparency around financial safeguards could become an important differentiator in the lettings market.

Chris Mason of The Letting Partnership noted: "Trust remains fundamental in landlord-agent relationships, especially when handling substantial sums and complex regulations. Our findings show that while most partnerships work well, there's clear demand for better evidence of proper financial controls."

Industry experts suggest that agents who proactively share compliance credentials and undergo external audits may gain a competitive advantage. Simple steps like clearly displaying Client Money Protection certification or explaining deposit protection arrangements could help address landlord concerns.

For landlords, these findings highlight the importance of asking agents about their financial safeguards. Checking whether an agent holds relevant certifications or undergoes independent reviews might provide valuable peace of mind when entrusting them with rental income and deposits.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that nearly 20% of landlords in England have concerns about letting agents' handling of rental payments and deposits. The study by The Letting Partnership, which surveyed 890 landlords, highlights ongoing trust issues in the rental sector despite most property owners generally trusting their agents.

Landlords seek greater financial transparency

The survey found that 19% of landlords lack confidence in agents' management of client money, including rent collection and deposit holding. This suggests that while the majority of working relationships function smoothly, a significant minority of property investors remain uneasy about financial processes.

Communication gaps appear to be a key factor, with 34% of respondents stating their agents fail to clearly explain compliance procedures or professional standards. The research also uncovered low awareness of voluntary industry checks, as 76% of landlords didn't know some agents undergo independent assessments of their financial and operational practices.

Independent verification could boost confidence

Nearly all surveyed landlords (99%) said they would feel more confident in an agent that could demonstrate independently verified compliance. This strong response indicates that transparency around financial safeguards could become an important differentiator in the lettings market.

Chris Mason of The Letting Partnership noted: "Trust remains fundamental in landlord-agent relationships, especially when handling substantial sums and complex regulations. Our findings show that while most partnerships work well, there's clear demand for better evidence of proper financial controls."

Industry experts suggest that agents who proactively share compliance credentials and undergo external audits may gain a competitive advantage. Simple steps like clearly displaying Client Money Protection certification or explaining deposit protection arrangements could help address landlord concerns.

For landlords, these findings highlight the importance of asking agents about their financial safeguards. Checking whether an agent holds relevant certifications or undergoes independent reviews might provide valuable peace of mind when entrusting them with rental income and deposits.

item.title: Propertymark backs tougher landlord registration rules
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Propertymark Supports Tougher Landlord Registration Rules in Northern Ireland

Propertymark has voiced its support for proposed changes to Northern Ireland's landlord registration system, stating that strengthened regulations could enhance enforcement against non-compliant landlords whilst maintaining tenant protections. The professional body responded to a consultation by the Department for Communities regarding updates to the 2014 Landlord Registration Scheme Regulations, endorsing measures that would require landlords to submit additional property information during registration.

The proposed reforms would mandate landlords to provide documentation including gas safety certificates, electrical installation condition reports, energy performance certificates, and evidence of working smoke and carbon monoxide alarms. Propertymark emphasised that these requirements could help identify properties failing to meet basic safety standards whilst creating a fairer marketplace for responsible landlords.

Balancing Compliance with Practicality

Whilst supporting the principle of tighter regulation, Propertymark cautioned that new requirements should remain proportionate and manageable for landlords and letting agents. Feedback from the organisation's Northern Ireland members indicated strong backing for mandatory gas safety certification in particular, with calls for the registration system to minimise duplication of existing compliance checks.

Henry Griffith, Policy Officer at Propertymark, commented: "A robust registration scheme can help distinguish between landlords maintaining proper standards and those cutting corners. The proposals could strengthen enforcement whilst giving tenants greater confidence in their rented accommodation."

The organisation also advocated for:

  • Clear guidance on letting agents' responsibilities in managing registration paperwork
  • Direct upload capabilities for agents submitting documents on landlords' behalf
  • Improved data sharing between enforcement bodies to target non-compliant properties

Propertymark's response highlights the delicate balance between raising standards in the private rented sector and avoiding excessive administrative burdens that might discourage landlord participation. The proposed changes to Northern Ireland's registration system could serve as a model for strengthening tenant protections without over-regulating responsible property owners.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Propertymark Supports Tougher Landlord Registration Rules in Northern Ireland

Propertymark has voiced its support for proposed changes to Northern Ireland's landlord registration system, stating that strengthened regulations could enhance enforcement against non-compliant landlords whilst maintaining tenant protections. The professional body responded to a consultation by the Department for Communities regarding updates to the 2014 Landlord Registration Scheme Regulations, endorsing measures that would require landlords to submit additional property information during registration.

The proposed reforms would mandate landlords to provide documentation including gas safety certificates, electrical installation condition reports, energy performance certificates, and evidence of working smoke and carbon monoxide alarms. Propertymark emphasised that these requirements could help identify properties failing to meet basic safety standards whilst creating a fairer marketplace for responsible landlords.

Balancing Compliance with Practicality

Whilst supporting the principle of tighter regulation, Propertymark cautioned that new requirements should remain proportionate and manageable for landlords and letting agents. Feedback from the organisation's Northern Ireland members indicated strong backing for mandatory gas safety certification in particular, with calls for the registration system to minimise duplication of existing compliance checks.

Henry Griffith, Policy Officer at Propertymark, commented: "A robust registration scheme can help distinguish between landlords maintaining proper standards and those cutting corners. The proposals could strengthen enforcement whilst giving tenants greater confidence in their rented accommodation."

The organisation also advocated for:

  • Clear guidance on letting agents' responsibilities in managing registration paperwork
  • Direct upload capabilities for agents submitting documents on landlords' behalf
  • Improved data sharing between enforcement bodies to target non-compliant properties

Propertymark's response highlights the delicate balance between raising standards in the private rented sector and avoiding excessive administrative burdens that might discourage landlord participation. The proposed changes to Northern Ireland's registration system could serve as a model for strengthening tenant protections without over-regulating responsible property owners.

item.title: Rightmove set to be demoted from FTSE 100
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove faces FTSE 100 exit amid share price struggles

Property portal Rightmove appears set for demotion from the FTSE 100 index later this month following a challenging year that has seen its share price decline by over 40%. According to FTSE Russell's latest review, the company is likely to move to the FTSE 250 when the quarterly reshuffle takes effect on 22 June. This potential downgrade marks a significant shift for what has been one of the UK's most prominent listed property businesses since its 2006 flotation.

Market turbulence and investor concerns

Rightmove's position in the blue-chip index has come under pressure from multiple fronts. The company recently announced a £60m investment in artificial intelligence capabilities while simultaneously facing activist shareholder pressure. These developments have coincided with a £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year, creating uncertainty for investors.

The property platform's shares reached record highs during the 2021 property market boom, achieving a market capitalisation of approximately £6.7bn. However, the company's rejection of multiple takeover approaches from Australia's REA Group in September 2024 - including a £6.2bn valuation - appears to have contributed to the subsequent share price decline. With the stock now trading well below REA's final offer price, Rightmove's current market valuation stands at around £3.3bn.

Broader implications for property sector

Rightmove's potential demotion comes alongside expectations that housebuilder Berkeley Group may also exit the FTSE 100, reflecting wider challenges in the property sector. Market observers might view these developments as indicators of changing conditions for property-related businesses, particularly those exposed to digital platforms and development markets.

For shareholders and market watchers, the coming weeks will reveal whether Rightmove can stabilise its position or whether this marks the beginning of a new chapter for the property listings giant outside the premier index. The company's future strategy and ability to adapt to changing market conditions may prove crucial in determining its longer-term trajectory.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove faces FTSE 100 exit amid share price struggles

Property portal Rightmove appears set for demotion from the FTSE 100 index later this month following a challenging year that has seen its share price decline by over 40%. According to FTSE Russell's latest review, the company is likely to move to the FTSE 250 when the quarterly reshuffle takes effect on 22 June. This potential downgrade marks a significant shift for what has been one of the UK's most prominent listed property businesses since its 2006 flotation.

Market turbulence and investor concerns

Rightmove's position in the blue-chip index has come under pressure from multiple fronts. The company recently announced a £60m investment in artificial intelligence capabilities while simultaneously facing activist shareholder pressure. These developments have coincided with a £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year, creating uncertainty for investors.

The property platform's shares reached record highs during the 2021 property market boom, achieving a market capitalisation of approximately £6.7bn. However, the company's rejection of multiple takeover approaches from Australia's REA Group in September 2024 - including a £6.2bn valuation - appears to have contributed to the subsequent share price decline. With the stock now trading well below REA's final offer price, Rightmove's current market valuation stands at around £3.3bn.

Broader implications for property sector

Rightmove's potential demotion comes alongside expectations that housebuilder Berkeley Group may also exit the FTSE 100, reflecting wider challenges in the property sector. Market observers might view these developments as indicators of changing conditions for property-related businesses, particularly those exposed to digital platforms and development markets.

For shareholders and market watchers, the coming weeks will reveal whether Rightmove can stabilise its position or whether this marks the beginning of a new chapter for the property listings giant outside the premier index. The company's future strategy and ability to adapt to changing market conditions may prove crucial in determining its longer-term trajectory.

item.title: MPs call on Reeves to scrap ‘economy-damaging’ stamp duty
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs are urging Chancellor Rachel Reeves to reconsider stamp duty reforms, warning that the current system may be harming housing affordability and economic growth. A cross-party committee has highlighted concerns that the tax creates barriers for first-time buyers whilst limiting mobility in the property market.

Growing Calls for Stamp Duty Reform

The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently published findings suggesting stamp duty in its current form could be counterproductive. Whilst acknowledging its importance as a Treasury revenue stream, the report argues the tax may inadvertently suppress housing market activity.

Key concerns raised include:

  • Reduced affordability for prospective homeowners
  • Restricted movement within the housing market
  • Potential negative impact on economic growth

The committee has recommended launching a consultation before year-end to explore alternatives, including potential replacements or adjustments to existing thresholds and reliefs.

Balancing Reform with Fiscal Responsibility

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, MPs stress the need for careful consideration of any changes. The report cautions against temporary measures, pointing to the post-pandemic stamp duty holiday which contributed to price inflation before demand tapered off.

Committee members emphasised that reforms should:

  • Form part of wider property tax reviews
  • Consider local housing market variations
  • Maintain long-term stability rather than offer short-term fixes

The debate comes as housing affordability continues to challenge many prospective buyers, particularly those without access to family financial support. Industry experts suggest any changes to stamp duty might need to be balanced against other taxation measures to avoid unintended market distortions.

As discussions continue, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for potential developments in property taxation policy. The government faces the complex task of balancing housing market stimulation with sustainable public finances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs are urging Chancellor Rachel Reeves to reconsider stamp duty reforms, warning that the current system may be harming housing affordability and economic growth. A cross-party committee has highlighted concerns that the tax creates barriers for first-time buyers whilst limiting mobility in the property market.

Growing Calls for Stamp Duty Reform

The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently published findings suggesting stamp duty in its current form could be counterproductive. Whilst acknowledging its importance as a Treasury revenue stream, the report argues the tax may inadvertently suppress housing market activity.

Key concerns raised include:

  • Reduced affordability for prospective homeowners
  • Restricted movement within the housing market
  • Potential negative impact on economic growth

The committee has recommended launching a consultation before year-end to explore alternatives, including potential replacements or adjustments to existing thresholds and reliefs.

Balancing Reform with Fiscal Responsibility

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, MPs stress the need for careful consideration of any changes. The report cautions against temporary measures, pointing to the post-pandemic stamp duty holiday which contributed to price inflation before demand tapered off.

Committee members emphasised that reforms should:

  • Form part of wider property tax reviews
  • Consider local housing market variations
  • Maintain long-term stability rather than offer short-term fixes

The debate comes as housing affordability continues to challenge many prospective buyers, particularly those without access to family financial support. Industry experts suggest any changes to stamp duty might need to be balanced against other taxation measures to avoid unintended market distortions.

As discussions continue, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for potential developments in property taxation policy. The government faces the complex task of balancing housing market stimulation with sustainable public finances.

item.title: Case updates from your lawyer – useful or not?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Do Legal Case Updates Really Improve Client Satisfaction?

A recent report examining common complaints made to the Legal Ombudsman has highlighted communication gaps between conveyancers and their clients. The findings suggest that regular updates – even when there's no progress to report – could significantly reduce frustrations during the property buying process.

The Communication Gap in Conveyancing

The research revealed that lack of communication tops the list of client grievances. Many homeowners reported feeling left in the dark during lengthy conveyancing processes, particularly when facing delays. While legal professionals might view "no news" as simply meaning no change, clients often interpret silence negatively.

Key findings include:

  • Most complaints stem from perceived lack of updates rather than legal errors
  • Clients value transparency about delays or inactive periods
  • Simple status updates could prevent many unnecessary complaints

Balancing Client Expectations With Legal Realities

The report suggests that conveyancers might benefit from setting clearer expectations at the outset. While daily updates may be impractical, establishing a regular communication schedule could help manage client expectations. Some firms have begun implementing:

  • Automated status notifications when waiting for third-party responses
  • Scheduled fortnightly update emails, even if just to confirm no progress
  • Online client portals showing case progress in real-time

For homeowners navigating the property market, understanding that conveyancing often involves unavoidable waiting periods might help temper frustrations. Meanwhile, legal professionals might consider whether modest adjustments to their communication practices could enhance client satisfaction without significantly increasing workloads.

As the property market continues to face pressures, improving the client experience during conveyancing could benefit all parties involved. While no system will eliminate delays entirely, better communication might at least make the waiting less stressful.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Do Legal Case Updates Really Improve Client Satisfaction?

A recent report examining common complaints made to the Legal Ombudsman has highlighted communication gaps between conveyancers and their clients. The findings suggest that regular updates – even when there's no progress to report – could significantly reduce frustrations during the property buying process.

The Communication Gap in Conveyancing

The research revealed that lack of communication tops the list of client grievances. Many homeowners reported feeling left in the dark during lengthy conveyancing processes, particularly when facing delays. While legal professionals might view "no news" as simply meaning no change, clients often interpret silence negatively.

Key findings include:

  • Most complaints stem from perceived lack of updates rather than legal errors
  • Clients value transparency about delays or inactive periods
  • Simple status updates could prevent many unnecessary complaints

Balancing Client Expectations With Legal Realities

The report suggests that conveyancers might benefit from setting clearer expectations at the outset. While daily updates may be impractical, establishing a regular communication schedule could help manage client expectations. Some firms have begun implementing:

  • Automated status notifications when waiting for third-party responses
  • Scheduled fortnightly update emails, even if just to confirm no progress
  • Online client portals showing case progress in real-time

For homeowners navigating the property market, understanding that conveyancing often involves unavoidable waiting periods might help temper frustrations. Meanwhile, legal professionals might consider whether modest adjustments to their communication practices could enhance client satisfaction without significantly increasing workloads.

As the property market continues to face pressures, improving the client experience during conveyancing could benefit all parties involved. While no system will eliminate delays entirely, better communication might at least make the waiting less stressful.

item.title: The government’s 1.5m new homes target? ‘Nonsense’, says shadow housing secretary
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Shadow Housing Secretary Criticises Government's Housing Target as "Nonsense"

The government's pledge to build 1.5 million new homes has been dismissed as "nonsense" by shadow housing secretary James Cleverly. Speaking on a recent industry podcast, the Conservative MP argued that ministers are prioritising headline targets over practical measures needed to boost housing supply. Cleverly suggested that simply repeating the 1.5 million homes figure would not translate into actual construction without proper support for the businesses and workforce delivering these projects.

Cleverly highlighted what he sees as a growing disconnect between political ambition and real-world delivery in the housing sector. He told listeners: "One of the problems with politics is everybody wants to be an architect. No one wants to be a builder." The shadow minister warned that without recognising and rewarding those actually constructing homes, the UK risks endless discussion without meaningful progress.

Construction Skills Crisis Threatens Housing Goals

The Conservative MP raised particular concerns about skills shortages in the construction industry, suggesting the UK has undervalued vocational careers for decades. Cleverly remarked: "As a society we have lost our respect for skillfulness," noting the cultural preference for university education over trades. He argued this attitude has contributed to labour shortages that now hinder housebuilding efforts across the country.

Cleverly drew parallels between the respect shown to armed forces personnel and how society views construction workers, questioning why both shouldn't receive similar recognition. His comments come as many construction firms report difficulties recruiting skilled tradespeople, from bricklayers to electricians, despite competitive wages in the sector.

Stamp Duty Reforms Proposed to Boost Market Activity

The shadow housing secretary also outlined Conservative plans to scrap stamp duty, describing the tax as a "drag anchor on the British economy" that affects both homebuyers' finances and their willingness to move. Cleverly suggested that reducing this barrier could stimulate wider economic activity beyond just property transactions.

He explained: "When people move, they do stuff. They have an extension, get some repair work done, redo a kitchen or bathroom." The MP noted that this ancillary spending typically benefits local small and medium-sized building firms rather than large national developers, potentially supporting regional economies across the UK.

As the housing debate continues, Cleverly's intervention highlights growing concerns about whether current policies can deliver the homes Britain needs, with skills shortages and taxation both emerging as key battlegrounds in the run-up to the next general election.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Shadow Housing Secretary Criticises Government's Housing Target as "Nonsense"

The government's pledge to build 1.5 million new homes has been dismissed as "nonsense" by shadow housing secretary James Cleverly. Speaking on a recent industry podcast, the Conservative MP argued that ministers are prioritising headline targets over practical measures needed to boost housing supply. Cleverly suggested that simply repeating the 1.5 million homes figure would not translate into actual construction without proper support for the businesses and workforce delivering these projects.

Cleverly highlighted what he sees as a growing disconnect between political ambition and real-world delivery in the housing sector. He told listeners: "One of the problems with politics is everybody wants to be an architect. No one wants to be a builder." The shadow minister warned that without recognising and rewarding those actually constructing homes, the UK risks endless discussion without meaningful progress.

Construction Skills Crisis Threatens Housing Goals

The Conservative MP raised particular concerns about skills shortages in the construction industry, suggesting the UK has undervalued vocational careers for decades. Cleverly remarked: "As a society we have lost our respect for skillfulness," noting the cultural preference for university education over trades. He argued this attitude has contributed to labour shortages that now hinder housebuilding efforts across the country.

Cleverly drew parallels between the respect shown to armed forces personnel and how society views construction workers, questioning why both shouldn't receive similar recognition. His comments come as many construction firms report difficulties recruiting skilled tradespeople, from bricklayers to electricians, despite competitive wages in the sector.

Stamp Duty Reforms Proposed to Boost Market Activity

The shadow housing secretary also outlined Conservative plans to scrap stamp duty, describing the tax as a "drag anchor on the British economy" that affects both homebuyers' finances and their willingness to move. Cleverly suggested that reducing this barrier could stimulate wider economic activity beyond just property transactions.

He explained: "When people move, they do stuff. They have an extension, get some repair work done, redo a kitchen or bathroom." The MP noted that this ancillary spending typically benefits local small and medium-sized building firms rather than large national developers, potentially supporting regional economies across the UK.

As the housing debate continues, Cleverly's intervention highlights growing concerns about whether current policies can deliver the homes Britain needs, with skills shortages and taxation both emerging as key battlegrounds in the run-up to the next general election.

item.title: Estate agents remain biggest source of conveyancing referrals
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agents have retained their position as the primary source of conveyancing referrals, with nearly a third of homebuyers selecting solicitors recommended by their agents, according to recent research. The study, conducted by Lyons Bowe Solicitors, surveyed 1,208 homeowners who purchased properties within the last two years. It revealed that 32% of respondents relied on estate agent recommendations when choosing a conveyancer, making agents the most influential source of new business for conveyancing firms.

The Role of Estate Agents in Conveyancing Choices

Despite the rise of online comparison tools, estate agents remain a trusted intermediary in the homebuying process. Their recommendations carry significant weight, particularly in a market where demand has softened and competition for conveyancing instructions has intensified. The research highlights that personal referrals from estate agents are more influential than online searches or price comparisons when it comes to selecting a conveyancer.

Friends and family also play a notable role, with 25% of buyers opting for conveyancers based on personal recommendations. In contrast, only 19% of respondents found their conveyancer through an online search. This suggests that trust and familiarity remain key factors in decision-making, even in an increasingly digital age.

What Influences Homebuyers’ Decisions?

The study also sheds light on the factors that sway homebuyers when choosing a conveyancer. Among those who considered multiple options, 38% cited recommendations as the most important factor in their decision. Cost was a priority for just 20% of respondents, while online reviews influenced only 11%. This underscores the value buyers place on trusted advice over price or digital feedback.

Paul Lyons, managing director at Lyons Bowe Solicitors, emphasised the importance of maintaining strong relationships between conveyancers and estate agents. He noted that estate agents are more likely to recommend firms they trust to deliver reliable and efficient services, which can help transactions proceed smoothly. “In a subdued market, where competition for instructions is fierce, these relationships are more crucial than ever,” he added.

The findings serve as a reminder of the pivotal role estate agents continue to play in the home-moving process. As the market evolves, their recommendations remain a key driver of conveyancing instructions, highlighting the enduring importance of trust and personal connections in the property sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agents have retained their position as the primary source of conveyancing referrals, with nearly a third of homebuyers selecting solicitors recommended by their agents, according to recent research. The study, conducted by Lyons Bowe Solicitors, surveyed 1,208 homeowners who purchased properties within the last two years. It revealed that 32% of respondents relied on estate agent recommendations when choosing a conveyancer, making agents the most influential source of new business for conveyancing firms.

The Role of Estate Agents in Conveyancing Choices

Despite the rise of online comparison tools, estate agents remain a trusted intermediary in the homebuying process. Their recommendations carry significant weight, particularly in a market where demand has softened and competition for conveyancing instructions has intensified. The research highlights that personal referrals from estate agents are more influential than online searches or price comparisons when it comes to selecting a conveyancer.

Friends and family also play a notable role, with 25% of buyers opting for conveyancers based on personal recommendations. In contrast, only 19% of respondents found their conveyancer through an online search. This suggests that trust and familiarity remain key factors in decision-making, even in an increasingly digital age.

What Influences Homebuyers’ Decisions?

The study also sheds light on the factors that sway homebuyers when choosing a conveyancer. Among those who considered multiple options, 38% cited recommendations as the most important factor in their decision. Cost was a priority for just 20% of respondents, while online reviews influenced only 11%. This underscores the value buyers place on trusted advice over price or digital feedback.

Paul Lyons, managing director at Lyons Bowe Solicitors, emphasised the importance of maintaining strong relationships between conveyancers and estate agents. He noted that estate agents are more likely to recommend firms they trust to deliver reliable and efficient services, which can help transactions proceed smoothly. “In a subdued market, where competition for instructions is fierce, these relationships are more crucial than ever,” he added.

The findings serve as a reminder of the pivotal role estate agents continue to play in the home-moving process. As the market evolves, their recommendations remain a key driver of conveyancing instructions, highlighting the enduring importance of trust and personal connections in the property sector.

item.title: What People Regret Throwing Away During Decluttering in London
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Many London homeowners embarking on a major decluttering spree later find themselves wishing they hadn't been quite so ruthless. Whether preparing for a house move, tackling a loft clearance, or simply craving more living space, residents across the capital frequently report regretting items they hastily discarded during the process.

Common Decluttering Regrets

Property owners often realise too late that certain possessions held more value than they initially thought. Typical regrets include:

  • Sentimental items like childhood memorabilia or family heirlooms
  • Vintage furniture that could have been restored or repurposed
  • Books and vinyl records that later become sought-after collectors' items
  • Quality tools or DIY equipment that need replacing for home improvements

The emotional attachment to belongings often becomes clearer after they're gone, particularly when clearing a property after major life events like relationship breakdowns or bereavement. Many find that what seemed like clutter in the moment actually represented important personal history.

Making Smarter Decluttering Decisions

For those considering a major clear-out, it could be worth implementing a more measured approach:

  • Creating a "maybe" pile to revisit after a cooling-off period
  • Researching whether items have financial value before disposal
  • Considering professional valuation for antiques or collectables
  • Exploring storage solutions for seasonal or occasional-use items

London's competitive property market means many residents live in compact spaces, making thoughtful decluttering particularly important. Whilst creating more living space remains valuable, keeping hold of meaningful or potentially valuable possessions might save future regrets.

Homeowners facing storage challenges might explore creative solutions like under-bed storage, wall-mounted shelving, or multi-functional furniture before opting for wholesale clearances. Taking time to assess each item's true worth could prevent that all-too-common decluttering remorse.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Many London homeowners embarking on a major decluttering spree later find themselves wishing they hadn't been quite so ruthless. Whether preparing for a house move, tackling a loft clearance, or simply craving more living space, residents across the capital frequently report regretting items they hastily discarded during the process.

Common Decluttering Regrets

Property owners often realise too late that certain possessions held more value than they initially thought. Typical regrets include:

  • Sentimental items like childhood memorabilia or family heirlooms
  • Vintage furniture that could have been restored or repurposed
  • Books and vinyl records that later become sought-after collectors' items
  • Quality tools or DIY equipment that need replacing for home improvements

The emotional attachment to belongings often becomes clearer after they're gone, particularly when clearing a property after major life events like relationship breakdowns or bereavement. Many find that what seemed like clutter in the moment actually represented important personal history.

Making Smarter Decluttering Decisions

For those considering a major clear-out, it could be worth implementing a more measured approach:

  • Creating a "maybe" pile to revisit after a cooling-off period
  • Researching whether items have financial value before disposal
  • Considering professional valuation for antiques or collectables
  • Exploring storage solutions for seasonal or occasional-use items

London's competitive property market means many residents live in compact spaces, making thoughtful decluttering particularly important. Whilst creating more living space remains valuable, keeping hold of meaningful or potentially valuable possessions might save future regrets.

Homeowners facing storage challenges might explore creative solutions like under-bed storage, wall-mounted shelving, or multi-functional furniture before opting for wholesale clearances. Taking time to assess each item's true worth could prevent that all-too-common decluttering remorse.

item.title: Rents rise in May but year-on-year growth remains subdued
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

May saw a modest increase in rental prices across the UK, though annual growth remains relatively low. This subdued trend comes as the market continues to adjust to the implications of the Renters’ Rights Act, introduced earlier this year. Landlords and tenants alike are navigating the changes, with the legislation aiming to provide greater protections for renters whilst balancing the interests of property investors.

Monthly Rent Increases

Data from recent reports highlights a slight uptick in rental prices during May. Whilst this rise may indicate a gradual recovery in the rental market, the increase remains marginal. Factors such as seasonal demand and a stabilising housing market could be contributing to this trend. However, the overall pace of growth suggests that the market is still finding its footing after a period of uncertainty.

Impact of the Renters’ Rights Act

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into effect earlier this year, has brought significant changes to the rental landscape. Designed to enhance tenant protections, the legislation includes measures such as stricter regulations on evictions and improved standards for rental properties. Whilst these changes are welcomed by tenants, landlords may find themselves adapting to new responsibilities and compliance requirements. This transitional phase could be influencing the slower annual growth in rental prices, as both parties adjust to the evolving framework.

Homeowners and property investors might consider reviewing their strategies in light of these developments. For landlords, ensuring compliance with the new regulations could help maintain tenant satisfaction and reduce potential disputes. Tenants, on the other hand, may benefit from understanding their enhanced rights under the Act, enabling them to make informed decisions when renting properties.

Looking Ahead

As the rental market continues to adapt, it remains to be seen how these trends will evolve in the coming months. Whilst monthly increases in rent suggest a degree of stability, the subdued annual growth indicates that the market is still in a state of flux. Both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about further developments, particularly as the long-term effects of the Renters’ Rights Act become clearer.

For those navigating the rental market, seeking professional advice could prove valuable. Mortgage brokers, letting agents, and legal advisors may provide insights tailored to individual circumstances, helping both landlords and tenants make the most of the current landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

May saw a modest increase in rental prices across the UK, though annual growth remains relatively low. This subdued trend comes as the market continues to adjust to the implications of the Renters’ Rights Act, introduced earlier this year. Landlords and tenants alike are navigating the changes, with the legislation aiming to provide greater protections for renters whilst balancing the interests of property investors.

Monthly Rent Increases

Data from recent reports highlights a slight uptick in rental prices during May. Whilst this rise may indicate a gradual recovery in the rental market, the increase remains marginal. Factors such as seasonal demand and a stabilising housing market could be contributing to this trend. However, the overall pace of growth suggests that the market is still finding its footing after a period of uncertainty.

Impact of the Renters’ Rights Act

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into effect earlier this year, has brought significant changes to the rental landscape. Designed to enhance tenant protections, the legislation includes measures such as stricter regulations on evictions and improved standards for rental properties. Whilst these changes are welcomed by tenants, landlords may find themselves adapting to new responsibilities and compliance requirements. This transitional phase could be influencing the slower annual growth in rental prices, as both parties adjust to the evolving framework.

Homeowners and property investors might consider reviewing their strategies in light of these developments. For landlords, ensuring compliance with the new regulations could help maintain tenant satisfaction and reduce potential disputes. Tenants, on the other hand, may benefit from understanding their enhanced rights under the Act, enabling them to make informed decisions when renting properties.

Looking Ahead

As the rental market continues to adapt, it remains to be seen how these trends will evolve in the coming months. Whilst monthly increases in rent suggest a degree of stability, the subdued annual growth indicates that the market is still in a state of flux. Both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about further developments, particularly as the long-term effects of the Renters’ Rights Act become clearer.

For those navigating the rental market, seeking professional advice could prove valuable. Mortgage brokers, letting agents, and legal advisors may provide insights tailored to individual circumstances, helping both landlords and tenants make the most of the current landscape.

item.title: London sees 69% premium on average rents
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

London's rental market continues to command significantly higher prices than the national average, with new figures revealing a striking 69% premium compared to typical UK rents. The capital's average monthly rent now stands at £2,339, reflecting sustained demand from tenants across all boroughs.

What's driving London's rental premium?

Several factors contribute to London's substantial rental premium:

  • Consistently high demand from professionals and international tenants
  • Limited housing stock relative to population growth
  • Premium prices for properties near transport hubs and business districts
  • Ongoing return-to-office trends boosting central London demand

Implications for renters and landlords

For tenants considering a move to the capital, these figures highlight the importance of budgeting carefully for housing costs. Those relocating for work might wish to explore:

  • Commuter towns with lower rents but good transport links
  • House shares to split costs in premium locations
  • Negotiating longer tenancies to lock in current rates

For landlords and property investors, London's rental market continues to present opportunities, though it's worth noting that higher rents often come with increased tenant expectations regarding property standards and location amenities.

As the market evolves, both renters and property owners might benefit from staying informed about local rental trends and considering professional advice when making housing decisions in the capital.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

London's rental market continues to command significantly higher prices than the national average, with new figures revealing a striking 69% premium compared to typical UK rents. The capital's average monthly rent now stands at £2,339, reflecting sustained demand from tenants across all boroughs.

What's driving London's rental premium?

Several factors contribute to London's substantial rental premium:

  • Consistently high demand from professionals and international tenants
  • Limited housing stock relative to population growth
  • Premium prices for properties near transport hubs and business districts
  • Ongoing return-to-office trends boosting central London demand

Implications for renters and landlords

For tenants considering a move to the capital, these figures highlight the importance of budgeting carefully for housing costs. Those relocating for work might wish to explore:

  • Commuter towns with lower rents but good transport links
  • House shares to split costs in premium locations
  • Negotiating longer tenancies to lock in current rates

For landlords and property investors, London's rental market continues to present opportunities, though it's worth noting that higher rents often come with increased tenant expectations regarding property standards and location amenities.

As the market evolves, both renters and property owners might benefit from staying informed about local rental trends and considering professional advice when making housing decisions in the capital.

item.title: Auctions generate four times number of complaints
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions are facing scrutiny as new data reveals they generate four times as many complaints as traditional property transactions. Industry experts are calling for improved transparency and clearer guidance for buyers navigating the fast-paced auction environment.

Understanding the complaint disparity

The significant gap in complaints between auction purchases and conventional property sales has raised eyebrows across the sector. Several factors may contribute to this trend:

  • The binding nature of auction contracts leaves little room for reconsideration after the hammer falls
  • Limited time for due diligence compared to standard property purchases
  • Potential misunderstandings about auction terms and conditions
  • The accelerated process may catch some buyers unprepared for the financial commitments

Moving towards better auction practices

Industry bodies are now examining ways to improve the auction experience whilst maintaining its efficiency. Some proposed measures include:

  • Standardised documentation to make terms clearer for all parties
  • Enhanced pre-auction information packs to help buyers make informed decisions
  • Better signposting of financial implications and timescales
  • Improved complaints handling procedures specific to auction transactions

For those considering property auctions, it could be worth thoroughly researching the process and seeking professional advice before participating. The speed and finality of auction purchases mean preparation is key to avoiding potential pitfalls.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions are facing scrutiny as new data reveals they generate four times as many complaints as traditional property transactions. Industry experts are calling for improved transparency and clearer guidance for buyers navigating the fast-paced auction environment.

Understanding the complaint disparity

The significant gap in complaints between auction purchases and conventional property sales has raised eyebrows across the sector. Several factors may contribute to this trend:

  • The binding nature of auction contracts leaves little room for reconsideration after the hammer falls
  • Limited time for due diligence compared to standard property purchases
  • Potential misunderstandings about auction terms and conditions
  • The accelerated process may catch some buyers unprepared for the financial commitments

Moving towards better auction practices

Industry bodies are now examining ways to improve the auction experience whilst maintaining its efficiency. Some proposed measures include:

  • Standardised documentation to make terms clearer for all parties
  • Enhanced pre-auction information packs to help buyers make informed decisions
  • Better signposting of financial implications and timescales
  • Improved complaints handling procedures specific to auction transactions

For those considering property auctions, it could be worth thoroughly researching the process and seeking professional advice before participating. The speed and finality of auction purchases mean preparation is key to avoiding potential pitfalls.

item.title: Chestertons Global launches new Marbella division
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Chestertons Global has announced the launch of a new division in Marbella, marking its latest expansion into the international luxury property market. The move comes as part of a strategic partnership aimed at catering to high-net-worth individuals and investors seeking premium real estate opportunities in the coveted Spanish coastal destination.

Focus on Marbella’s Luxury Market

Marbella, renowned for its glamorous lifestyle and picturesque Mediterranean setting, has long been a hotspot for luxury property buyers. Chestertons Global’s new division will focus on meeting the growing demand for high-end homes in the area, offering tailored services to clients looking to purchase or invest in exclusive properties. This expansion reflects the firm’s commitment to strengthening its presence in key international markets.

Partnership to Enhance Services

The launch of the Marbella division is the result of a collaboration with a local partner, combining Chestertons Global’s international expertise with in-depth regional knowledge. This partnership is expected to provide clients with seamless access to a wide range of luxury properties, from beachfront villas to modern penthouses, alongside expert advice on market trends and investment opportunities.

For homeowners or investors considering Marbella as a destination, working with an established agency like Chestertons Global could offer peace of mind and access to a diverse portfolio of premium properties. The firm’s reputation for delivering personalised service and its global network may prove valuable for those navigating the luxury real estate market.

As Chestertons Global continues to expand its footprint, its new Marbella division highlights the enduring appeal of the Costa del Sol for international buyers and investors. With its blend of natural beauty, upscale amenities, and strong investment potential, Marbella remains a prime location for luxury property transactions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Chestertons Global has announced the launch of a new division in Marbella, marking its latest expansion into the international luxury property market. The move comes as part of a strategic partnership aimed at catering to high-net-worth individuals and investors seeking premium real estate opportunities in the coveted Spanish coastal destination.

Focus on Marbella’s Luxury Market

Marbella, renowned for its glamorous lifestyle and picturesque Mediterranean setting, has long been a hotspot for luxury property buyers. Chestertons Global’s new division will focus on meeting the growing demand for high-end homes in the area, offering tailored services to clients looking to purchase or invest in exclusive properties. This expansion reflects the firm’s commitment to strengthening its presence in key international markets.

Partnership to Enhance Services

The launch of the Marbella division is the result of a collaboration with a local partner, combining Chestertons Global’s international expertise with in-depth regional knowledge. This partnership is expected to provide clients with seamless access to a wide range of luxury properties, from beachfront villas to modern penthouses, alongside expert advice on market trends and investment opportunities.

For homeowners or investors considering Marbella as a destination, working with an established agency like Chestertons Global could offer peace of mind and access to a diverse portfolio of premium properties. The firm’s reputation for delivering personalised service and its global network may prove valuable for those navigating the luxury real estate market.

As Chestertons Global continues to expand its footprint, its new Marbella division highlights the enduring appeal of the Costa del Sol for international buyers and investors. With its blend of natural beauty, upscale amenities, and strong investment potential, Marbella remains a prime location for luxury property transactions.

item.title: Major estate agency strikes fresh acquisition deal
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Dexters expands London footprint with Fraser & Co acquisition

One of London's largest estate agency groups has strengthened its presence across prime property markets with the acquisition of long-established firm Fraser & Co. The deal adds four London offices and a Hong Kong operation to Dexters' growing network, bringing over 30 experienced employees into the fold.

Strategic expansion in key locations

The acquisition sees Dexters absorb Fraser & Co's offices in some of London's most desirable postcodes, including the West End, City & Shoreditch areas, alongside Kew Bridge & Brentford and Colindale locations. The Paddington-based property management division also transfers as part of the deal.

For homeowners and investors in these areas, the merger could mean access to Dexters' wider resources whilst maintaining Fraser & Co's local market expertise. The Hong Kong connection may particularly interest international buyers looking at London property, as it strengthens Dexters' overseas service capabilities.

Combining decades of property expertise

Founded in 1995, Fraser & Co has built a strong reputation in prime London markets and new-build developments. Their integration into Dexters creates one of the capital's most extensive property networks, with the combined entity now operating from over 180 offices and managing 50,000 rental properties.

Dexters CEO Ash Kashyap highlighted Fraser & Co's journey from family business to respected industry player, stating the acquisition aligns with their strategy of combining local knowledge with corporate resources. Meanwhile, Fraser & Co's managing director Robert Fraser pointed to Dexters' market-leading position as a key factor in the decision to join forces.

For those considering property transactions in affected areas, the merger may bring benefits through enhanced services and potentially greater market coverage. However, as with any major industry consolidation, borrowers and sellers might wish to monitor how the changes affect local competition and service offerings.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Dexters expands London footprint with Fraser & Co acquisition

One of London's largest estate agency groups has strengthened its presence across prime property markets with the acquisition of long-established firm Fraser & Co. The deal adds four London offices and a Hong Kong operation to Dexters' growing network, bringing over 30 experienced employees into the fold.

Strategic expansion in key locations

The acquisition sees Dexters absorb Fraser & Co's offices in some of London's most desirable postcodes, including the West End, City & Shoreditch areas, alongside Kew Bridge & Brentford and Colindale locations. The Paddington-based property management division also transfers as part of the deal.

For homeowners and investors in these areas, the merger could mean access to Dexters' wider resources whilst maintaining Fraser & Co's local market expertise. The Hong Kong connection may particularly interest international buyers looking at London property, as it strengthens Dexters' overseas service capabilities.

Combining decades of property expertise

Founded in 1995, Fraser & Co has built a strong reputation in prime London markets and new-build developments. Their integration into Dexters creates one of the capital's most extensive property networks, with the combined entity now operating from over 180 offices and managing 50,000 rental properties.

Dexters CEO Ash Kashyap highlighted Fraser & Co's journey from family business to respected industry player, stating the acquisition aligns with their strategy of combining local knowledge with corporate resources. Meanwhile, Fraser & Co's managing director Robert Fraser pointed to Dexters' market-leading position as a key factor in the decision to join forces.

For those considering property transactions in affected areas, the merger may bring benefits through enhanced services and potentially greater market coverage. However, as with any major industry consolidation, borrowers and sellers might wish to monitor how the changes affect local competition and service offerings.

item.title: Ground rent cap to be brought forward by a year
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government has announced plans to fast-track its proposed ground rent cap, with the £250 limit now expected to take effect in late 2027 – a full year earlier than initially proposed. This acceleration comes amid mounting political pressure and follows recommendations from parliamentary committees calling for swifter leasehold reforms to protect homeowners.

What the changes mean for leaseholders

The forthcoming legislation, currently in draft form, aims to significantly reduce financial burdens for leasehold property owners across England and Wales. Under the proposals:

  • Ground rents would be capped at £250 annually
  • Future increases would be effectively frozen in real terms
  • Leaseholders could save thousands over the duration of their leases

The reforms form part of a broader package of measures designed to rebalance the relationship between leaseholders and freeholders. Housing experts suggest this represents the most substantial intervention in the leasehold sector for decades.

Political momentum behind the reforms

The revised timetable follows sustained pressure from backbench MPs and housing campaigners, with the Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently urging ministers to implement changes without delay. Key developments include:

  • The draft Bill published in January 2024
  • Plans to introduce final legislation before Parliament's summer recess
  • Cross-party support for faster implementation of leasehold reforms

A government spokesperson confirmed the accelerated schedule, stating the measures would deliver meaningful financial relief to millions of homeowners currently facing escalating ground rent charges.

With the legislation now moving through Parliament, leaseholders may wish to monitor developments closely, as these reforms could substantially alter the long-term costs associated with leasehold properties. The changes are particularly significant for those considering property purchases or lease extensions in the coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government has announced plans to fast-track its proposed ground rent cap, with the £250 limit now expected to take effect in late 2027 – a full year earlier than initially proposed. This acceleration comes amid mounting political pressure and follows recommendations from parliamentary committees calling for swifter leasehold reforms to protect homeowners.

What the changes mean for leaseholders

The forthcoming legislation, currently in draft form, aims to significantly reduce financial burdens for leasehold property owners across England and Wales. Under the proposals:

  • Ground rents would be capped at £250 annually
  • Future increases would be effectively frozen in real terms
  • Leaseholders could save thousands over the duration of their leases

The reforms form part of a broader package of measures designed to rebalance the relationship between leaseholders and freeholders. Housing experts suggest this represents the most substantial intervention in the leasehold sector for decades.

Political momentum behind the reforms

The revised timetable follows sustained pressure from backbench MPs and housing campaigners, with the Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently urging ministers to implement changes without delay. Key developments include:

  • The draft Bill published in January 2024
  • Plans to introduce final legislation before Parliament's summer recess
  • Cross-party support for faster implementation of leasehold reforms

A government spokesperson confirmed the accelerated schedule, stating the measures would deliver meaningful financial relief to millions of homeowners currently facing escalating ground rent charges.

With the legislation now moving through Parliament, leaseholders may wish to monitor developments closely, as these reforms could substantially alter the long-term costs associated with leasehold properties. The changes are particularly significant for those considering property purchases or lease extensions in the coming years.

item.title: Housing minister to deliver government housing update at Propertymark One
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property professionals may wish to note that Baroness Taylor, the government’s housing spokesperson in the House of Lords, is set to deliver a keynote address at the Propertymark One conference this Friday. The event at London’s ExCeL will provide insights into upcoming housing policy reforms and their potential impact on the UK property market.

Key Policy Updates for the Property Sector

As Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Housing and Local Government, Baroness Taylor is expected to outline the government’s current priorities for the housing market. These may include:

  • Proposed changes to the home buying and selling process
  • New requirements for material information in property listings
  • Ongoing regulatory reforms affecting estate agents and conveyancers

The conference comes at a time when the property sector faces significant transformation, with technological advancements and shifting consumer expectations driving change across the industry.

Wider Conference Programme Highlights

Alongside the ministerial update, Propertymark One will feature discussions on several pressing industry topics:

  • Improving transaction speeds in the property market
  • The growing role of AI and proptech in real estate
  • Emerging business models for estate agencies
  • Current recruitment challenges in the sector

Other notable speakers include business figures Karren Brady and Daisy McAndrew, alongside politicians Mel Stride and Angela Rayner, making this a significant gathering for property professionals seeking to understand future market directions.

With housing policy reforms potentially affecting everything from mortgage applications to property transactions, industry professionals might consider such events valuable for staying informed about upcoming changes. The conference represents an opportunity to gain direct insight into government thinking at a time of considerable evolution for the UK property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property professionals may wish to note that Baroness Taylor, the government’s housing spokesperson in the House of Lords, is set to deliver a keynote address at the Propertymark One conference this Friday. The event at London’s ExCeL will provide insights into upcoming housing policy reforms and their potential impact on the UK property market.

Key Policy Updates for the Property Sector

As Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Housing and Local Government, Baroness Taylor is expected to outline the government’s current priorities for the housing market. These may include:

  • Proposed changes to the home buying and selling process
  • New requirements for material information in property listings
  • Ongoing regulatory reforms affecting estate agents and conveyancers

The conference comes at a time when the property sector faces significant transformation, with technological advancements and shifting consumer expectations driving change across the industry.

Wider Conference Programme Highlights

Alongside the ministerial update, Propertymark One will feature discussions on several pressing industry topics:

  • Improving transaction speeds in the property market
  • The growing role of AI and proptech in real estate
  • Emerging business models for estate agencies
  • Current recruitment challenges in the sector

Other notable speakers include business figures Karren Brady and Daisy McAndrew, alongside politicians Mel Stride and Angela Rayner, making this a significant gathering for property professionals seeking to understand future market directions.

With housing policy reforms potentially affecting everything from mortgage applications to property transactions, industry professionals might consider such events valuable for staying informed about upcoming changes. The conference represents an opportunity to gain direct insight into government thinking at a time of considerable evolution for the UK property market.

item.title: More than half of first-time buyers rely on family cash
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New research from Savills reveals that over half of first-time buyers in the UK now depend on financial assistance from family members to purchase their first home. The study highlights how rising property prices and higher mortgage rates have made it increasingly difficult for new entrants to the housing market without family support.

The growing role of family support

According to the findings, 53% of first-time buyers received some form of financial help from relatives in 2025, with family contributions totalling an estimated £11bn. This includes £8.3bn in direct financial support, demonstrating the significant role played by what's often termed the 'Bank of Mum and Dad'.

The research shows that gifts remain the most common form of assistance, with 32% of buyers receiving money from family members. Meanwhile, 16% relied on loans from relatives, and 14% used inherited wealth to fund their purchase. Interestingly, support is increasingly coming from extended family networks rather than just parents.

Challenges in the current market

With higher mortgage rates stretching affordability, the average deposit required remains substantial. Savills notes that while 64% of first-time buyers still use their own savings towards deposits, family assistance has become crucial for many to bridge the gap.

Lucian Cook, Savills' head of residential research, commented: "First-time buyer activity has remained more resilient than anticipated, but the outlook remains challenging in the current interest rate environment. While gradual rate reductions and less stringent mortgage regulation may improve access to homeownership, family support will likely continue playing a vital role."

The research also found that only 12% of first-time buyers used government-backed homeownership schemes, suggesting these initiatives may not be reaching as many potential homeowners as intended.

As the property market continues to evolve, first-time buyers might consider exploring all available options, from family assistance to government schemes, when planning their route onto the property ladder.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New research from Savills reveals that over half of first-time buyers in the UK now depend on financial assistance from family members to purchase their first home. The study highlights how rising property prices and higher mortgage rates have made it increasingly difficult for new entrants to the housing market without family support.

The growing role of family support

According to the findings, 53% of first-time buyers received some form of financial help from relatives in 2025, with family contributions totalling an estimated £11bn. This includes £8.3bn in direct financial support, demonstrating the significant role played by what's often termed the 'Bank of Mum and Dad'.

The research shows that gifts remain the most common form of assistance, with 32% of buyers receiving money from family members.
